description:
You are a platform engineer reviewing nightly build logs saved by your CI/CD pipeline.  
    Three raw log files are already present in /home/user/ci_logs/:

        • /home/user/ci_logs/build_backend_20230912.log  
        • /home/user/ci_logs/build_frontend_20230912.log  
        • /home/user/ci_logs/build_docs_20230912.log  

    Goal:  
      1. For every “.log” file in /home/user/ci_logs, use awk and/or sed (no other parsing tools) to extract only the lines that contain the substring “ERROR” (case-sensitive).  
      2. Place those extracted lines, in the same order they appeared in the source file, in a new file whose name is the original file name plus “.errors” (e.g. build_backend_20230912.log → build_backend_20230912.log.errors).  
      3. Store all “.errors” files inside a newly created directory /home/user/ci_logs/trimmed_logs/.  
      4. Produce a tab-separated summary file called /home/user/ci_logs/error_summary.tsv with the following exact format (including the header line and TAB characters):  

            filename<TAB>error_count
            build_backend_20230912.log<TAB><integer>
            build_frontend_20230912.log<TAB><integer>
            build_docs_20230912.log<TAB><integer>

         where <integer> is the number of ERROR-containing lines in the corresponding source file.  
      5. Ensure error_summary.tsv is sorted alphabetically by the original log file name (already reflected in the example layout above).  

    Verification notes:  
       • “trimmed_logs” directory must exist after you run the commands.  
       • Every “*.log.errors” file must exist and contain only ERROR lines—no blank lines, no INFO lines.  
       • error_summary.tsv must match the exact header and TAB-separated layout shown above; each numeric count must be correct.  
       • Use awk and/or sed only (grep, perl, python, etc. are not allowed).  
       • Do NOT move or modify the original .log files; create new artifacts as described.  

    A quick sanity-check for yourself after finishing:  

        $ cat /home/user/ci_logs/error_summary.tsv
        filename  <TAB> error_count
        ... etc.

    Follow good CLI practices—use relative or absolute paths as you prefer, but ensure the resulting files sit at the exact paths given so automated tests can locate them.
